name: Annotation Intent
summary: Select content then attach a motivation payload (highlighting, commenting, tagging, suggestion) with an overlay disposition. UX-only: the durable target is delegated to the range-anchor contract; the suggestion motivation's body is delegated to the suggested-edit contract — this intent owns no anchor machinery and no host mutation. Orphaned annotations (the anchored text is gone) are a first-class outcome; comment-thread product UI stays app-level.

motivation.description: Why the content was annotated — the W3C Web Annotation motivation vocabulary, the payload's discriminant

disposition.description: How the annotation is presented over the host content

surface.description: Whether the annotation overlays read-only content or becomes an in-model mark in editable content (composes the rich-text intent when editable)

anchorState.description: Lifecycle of the durable target (resolved through the range-anchor contract); orphaned is a first-class outcome, never an error

description:
Motivation Payload

The user selects content, then attaches a payload whose motivation follows the W3C Web Annotation vocabulary.

  • highlighting: Mark a passage as salient. No body text required.
  • commenting: Attach a free-text note to the selection.
  • tagging: Attach one or more labels/keywords.
  • suggestion: Propose an edit to the selected text (the Google Docs / suggestion-mode shape).

Disposition

How the annotation renders over the host content.

  • highlight: Paint the range via the CSS Custom Highlight API — no DOM mutation of the host.
  • inline-mark: Wrap the range in a styled mark (only when the host is editable — composes the Rich Text Intent's in-model marks).
  • margin: A gutter note aligned to the anchored line.
  • popover: An anchored surface (composes the Anchor Intent) shown on hover/activation.

Surface

overlay annotates read-only content non-destructively (Custom Highlight API + the durable anchor); in-model writes a real mark into editable content via the rich-text model. The same motivation payload rides either surface.

Anchoring & Orphans

The where — capturing the selection and re-resolving it after the document is re-fetched or lightly edited — is not owned here. It is delegated to the durable range-anchor contract (we:blocks/range-anchor/contract.ts, #1471), which stores a W3C selector bundle and re-resolves with a fuzzy fallback. When the anchored text is gone, the annotation enters orphaned — a first-class outcome the UX must represent (e.g. an orphan tray), never a silent drop or an error. The selection capture itself composes the Selection Intent.

Scope boundary

This intent declares the what (motivation + disposition + surface + orphan handling). It owns no anchor machinery (range-anchor), no positioning math (the anchor/positioning provider), and no comment-thread product UI (reply chains, mentions, resolution workflow) — those stay app-level above the intent.

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: The motivation vocabulary (highlighting/commenting/tagging/editing) is the upstream standard, adopted wholesale; the target selectors are owned by the range-anchor contract. We standardize the UX of attaching a motivation body, not the wire model.
Production annotators treat the orphaned-annotation state as first-class (an 'orphans' bucket), confirming orphan handling belongs in the intent surface, not as an error path.
Native, range-based painting with zero host-DOM mutation — the default for the read-only overlay disposition, leaving inline-mark for the editable (rich-text) surface only.
The suggestion motivation and the margin disposition map directly; their in-document marks validate the overlay-vs-in-model surface split (editable docs write marks; read-only viewers overlay).

researchGaps: Multi-range / discontinuous selections — does a single annotation payload attach to one range bundle or a set? The range-anchor contract anchors one range; confirm whether annotation composes N anchors or defers multi-range to the app.
Orphan re-anchoring UX — when a fuzzy re-resolve offers a candidate, is the confirm/reject affordance part of this intent or app-level?
